planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost, or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, average to poor, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade after last frost; mealy cup sage blooms best with at least 6 hours of direct sun.

directions: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost or direct sow after last frost in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade; mealy cup sage is a Texas and New Mexico native that produces elegant, white-felted stems topped with intensely blue-violet flower spikes from late spring through hard frost — one of the longest-blooming, most butterfly-attractive perennials for southern and Gulf Coast gardens. The species name 'farinacea' refers to the white, flour-like (farinose) coating on the calyx and stem. Drought-tolerant and deer-resistant once established. Shear spent flower stalks by half in midsummer to refresh growth and extend bloom. In zones 8–10 it overwinters as a woody-based subshrub; in cooler zones grow as an annual. 'Victoria Blue' (deep blue) and 'White Porcelain' are classic cultivar selections.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in late summer or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, average to poor, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; autumn sage is one of the finest and most hummingbird-attractive natives for zone 7–10 gardens.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in late summer or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to poor, well-drained soil in full sun; autumn sage is a compact, woody-based native of Texas and the Mexican mountains that blooms nearly year-round in mild climates — producing clusters of tubular flowers in brilliant red, cherry-pink, coral, salmon, or white that are magnets for ruby-throated hummingbirds and butterflies from spring through late fall. It tolerates extreme drought, summer heat, and alkaline rocky soil with negligible care. Cut back by one-half in late winter to promote dense, floriferous new growth. Among the most reliably ornamental and wildlife-valuable natives for dry Texas and Gulf Coast landscapes. Named varieties include 'Furman's Red' (large, intense red), 'Raspberry Royal', 'Cold Hardy Pink', and 'Wild Thing'.

planting.note: Direct sow outdoors after last frost or start indoors 6–8 weeks early; tropical sage self-sows prolifically in Gulf South gardens and often naturalized colonies require no replanting after the first year.

directions: Direct sow or start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost in average, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; tropical sage is a native of the southeastern US, Caribbean, and Mexico that produces a continuous display of loose spikes of two-lipped, brilliant scarlet flowers from late spring through frost — it is one of the finest hummingbird and butterfly plants available for southern gardens and thrives in partial shade where most salvias fail. Plants often self-sow freely to naturalize in Gulf South landscapes, maintaining a persistent colony that renews itself annually. 'Lady in Red' (compact, pure red) won an All-America Selections award; 'Coral Nymph' (bicolor coral and white) and 'Snow Nymph' (white) are popular alternatives. Deer-resistant.

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in late spring or plant container-grown specimens in moist to average, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; false indigo bush tolerates a wide range of harsh conditions including flooding, clay, and drought.

directions: Root softwood cuttings or plant container specimens in moist to average, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; false indigo bush is a large, fast-growing native deciduous shrub with fragrant, pinnate foliage that produces densely packed, upright, deep purple-indigo flower spikes in late spring that are highly attractive to native bees — particularly bumblebees, which are the primary pollinators. It fixes nitrogen through root nodules and significantly improves soil fertility. Excellent for stream banks, rain gardens, wet woodlands, and difficult wet-to-dry sites where few shrubs thrive. The dried seed pods have ornamental value through winter. The larval host of the Southern Dogface and several other butterflies. Can sucker to form thickets; useful for erosion control on slopes and streambanks.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in poor to average, well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; standing cypress requires a cold-winter rosette period before bolting and flowering the second year — fall sowing is essential for proper development.

directions: Direct sow in fall in poor to average, well-drained, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; standing cypress (Texas plume) is a breathtaking native biennial of the southeastern and south-central US that forms a low, ferny rosette of finely cut, cypress-like foliage in the first year, then bolts in the second year to send up a tall, stately spike packed densely with brilliant scarlet, tubular flowers from late spring to midsummer — one of the most spectacular plants for attracting hummingbirds in the Southeast and Gulf South. Sow fall seed in a nursery area and transplant rosettes in fall or early spring to their final position. Plants self-sow freely to maintain a colony once established, so only one initial seeding is typically necessary. Thrives in hot, dry conditions that discourage most flowers.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination, or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, average to poor, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; foxglove beardtongue adapts to a wide range of conditions including clay and seasonally wet soil.

directions: Direct sow in fall or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, average to poor soil in partial shade to full sun; foxglove beardtongue is the most adaptable and widely grown native penstemon in eastern North America, producing tall spikes of white, occasionally pale-pink, foxglove-like flowers in late spring to early summer that are valuable for native bumblebees, which must squeeze inside the flowers to reach the nectar — a striking spectacle of bee behavior. The glossy, semi-evergreen basal rosette and reddish-maroon winter foliage provide year-round interest. Excellent in moist meadows, prairie plantings, and partially shaded woodland edges. 'Husker Red' (intense maroon-purple foliage throughout the season, white flowers) is the most popular cultivar, earning a Perennial Plant of the Year award in 1996 — it delivers bold foliage interest even when not in bloom.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
